title: NCP-GENL · Evaluation
description: Metrics, LLM-as-judge, contamination, RAG faithfulness, benchmarks, error analysis (exam domain, 7%).

cards.front: Which metric is most appropriate to evaluate the faithfulness of a RAG-generated answer?
cards.back: An NLI-based or LLM-as-judge faithfulness score: each generated claim should be entailed by the retrieved context. Faithfulness is the property that the answer follows from the source. RAGAS, TruLens, and ARES operationalize this with natural-language-inference models or judge-LLM rubrics, often scored per individual claim.
cards.quizAnswer: An NLI-based or LLM-as-judge faithfulness score checking that each generated claim is entailed by the retrieved context.
distractors: BLEU against a reference answer, since n-gram overlap with the gold response measures how grounded the output is.
Inference latency per token, because faithful answers are produced by models that decode slowly and deliberately.
Perplexity of the answer under the base model, since low perplexity indicates the claims follow from the context.
An LLM-as-judge fluency score, which rates how natural the answer reads and therefore how well it matches the source.
ROUGE-L recall against the retrieved chunks, since longest-common-subsequence overlap proves the claims are entailed.
Retrieval recall@k, because if the right documents were retrieved the generated answer is necessarily faithful to them.

cards.front: What are the known significant biases of LLM-as-judge evaluation?
cards.back: Position bias (favoring the first or last option in a pairwise comparison), length bias (favoring verbose answers), and self-preference (favoring answers from the same model family). Mitigations: randomize positions and require consistency under swap, length-normalize, ensemble judges across families, and calibrate against a human-labeled subset with chain-of-thought rubrics.
cards.quizAnswer: Position bias (favoring first/last), length bias (favoring verbose answers), and self-preference for same-family models.
distractors: A strong bias toward shorter, less detailed answers, plus a tendency to penalize responses from the judge's own family.
No measurable biases, since a sufficiently capable judge model evaluates every response on its objective merits.
Uniformly random scoring, because judge models cannot distinguish quality differences between similar candidate answers.
Position bias and length bias, but never self-preference, since judges have no way to recognize their own family's style.
A bias toward answers that cite sources and a bias against answers written in the first person, regardless of order.
Topic bias only, where the judge favors domains it was trained on, with answer order and length having no effect.

cards.front: Why is benchmark contamination a serious concern?
cards.back: Pretraining or fine-tuning corpora may contain near-duplicates of public benchmark items, inflating reported scores while providing no real-world signal — the model has effectively memorized the test. Standard practice: run decontamination (n-gram or MinHash overlap with eval sets) on training data, and complement public benchmarks with private, domain-specific evals.
cards.quizAnswer: Training data may contain near-duplicates of benchmark items, inflating scores with no real signal — the model memorized the test.
distractors: It only affects reproducibility across hardware, since contaminated runs produce slightly different scores on different GPUs.
It speeds up training by giving the model easy examples, so contaminated models converge faster but score no higher.
It is irrelevant for closed models, because their training data is private and therefore cannot overlap public benchmarks.
Training data may contain near-duplicates of benchmark items, which deflates scores by confusing the model at test time.
It causes the tokenizer to overfit to benchmark formatting, so scores drop whenever the eval phrasing changes slightly.
It mainly inflates latency benchmarks, since memorized prompts are cached and answered faster than novel ones.

cards.front: Which benchmark is commonly used for instruction-following evaluation?
cards.back: IFEval (Instruction-Following Eval). It measures whether a model precisely follows verifiable instructions — constraints on length, format, and content (e.g., 'respond in exactly three bullet points'). It is a common signal for chat-tuned models alongside MT-Bench, MMLU, GSM8K, and HumanEval. ImageNet, TPC-H, and COCO are vision/database benchmarks, not LLM instruction-following.
cards.quizAnswer: IFEval — measures whether a model precisely follows verifiable instructions about length, format, and content constraints.
distractors: ImageNet, the large-scale image-classification benchmark, adapted to score how well an LLM follows text instructions.
TPC-H, the database query benchmark, which measures how precisely a model follows structured-output formatting rules.
COCO, the object-detection benchmark, repurposed to evaluate multi-constraint instruction following in chat models.
GSM8K, which measures instruction following by checking whether the model formats its grade-school math answers correctly.
MMLU, since its multiple-choice format directly tests whether a model follows the instruction to select one option.
HumanEval, because passing its unit tests requires the model to follow the docstring's instructions exactly.

cards.front: What is the most common cause of an offline-versus-online quality gap?
cards.back: The train/eval distribution doesn't match production traffic, or the eval set is contaminated or stale. The fix is to sample real production prompts (privacy-aware, PII-scrubbed, labeled) into a refreshed eval set, measure stratified by user segment, and instrument feedback channels (thumbs, edit-distance to user revisions) to keep the offline metric predictive of online behavior.
cards.quizAnswer: The eval distribution doesn't match production traffic (or the set is stale/contaminated); refresh it with sampled real prompts.
distractors: The model file is corrupted during deployment, so the served weights differ slightly from those evaluated offline.
Triton overrides the model's quality settings at serving time, applying a different sampling config than the eval harness.
Quantization always degrades quality at serving time regardless of method, so the online score is necessarily lower.
The eval distribution doesn't match production, but the only fix is retraining, since refreshing the eval set changes nothing.
Network latency between the client and server lowers answer quality, since rushed decoding produces worse completions.
The offline harness uses greedy decoding while production samples, so the gap closes by setting temperature to zero online.

cards.front: Match these quantitative metrics to their primary use: perplexity, BLEU, ROUGE-L, pass@k.
cards.back: Perplexity (exp of cross-entropy) measures language-modeling quality — lower is better. BLEU scores translation by n-gram precision against references. ROUGE-L scores summarization by longest-common-subsequence recall. pass@k (HumanEval/MBPP) measures code-generation accuracy — the fraction of problems solved within k samples. Using the wrong metric (e.g., BLEU for faithfulness) is a common trap.
cards.quizAnswer: Perplexity: LM quality; BLEU: translation n-gram precision; ROUGE-L: summarization LCS recall; pass@k: code-generation accuracy.
distractors: Perplexity: translation quality; BLEU: summarization recall; ROUGE-L: code accuracy; pass@k: language-modeling fit.
Perplexity: code accuracy; BLEU: language-modeling quality; ROUGE-L: translation precision; pass@k: summarization recall.
All four measure overlap with a reference answer; they differ only in whether they count unigrams, bigrams, or subsequences.
Perplexity: summarization recall; BLEU: code accuracy; ROUGE-L: language-modeling quality; pass@k: translation precision.
Perplexity: LM quality; BLEU: code accuracy via unit tests; ROUGE-L: translation precision; pass@k: summarization recall.
Perplexity and pass@k both measure factuality, while BLEU and ROUGE-L both measure latency under different decoding strategies.

cards.front: Which evaluation frameworks are standard for running LLM benchmarks, and what is each?
cards.back: NeMo Evaluator is NVIDIA's benchmarking harness (MMLU, HumanEval, and more). lm-evaluation-harness (EleutherAI) is the open-source multi-benchmark runner. LLM-as-judge uses a strong model to rate outputs against rubrics for open-ended quality. Human evaluation remains the gold standard for final validation but is expensive, so it is reserved for the last mile.
cards.quizAnswer: NeMo Evaluator (NVIDIA harness), lm-evaluation-harness (EleutherAI, open-source), LLM-as-judge for rubrics, human eval for final sign-off.
distractors: NeMo Curator for benchmarking, RAGAS for general MMLU runs, and DCGM for measuring per-task accuracy across the cluster.
lm-evaluation-harness for training only, NeMo Evaluator for serving metrics, and human eval for measuring latency SLAs.
TensorRT-LLM for benchmark execution, Triton Model Analyzer for accuracy scoring, and NIM for human-preference collection.
NeMo Evaluator for open-ended rubric scoring only, with lm-evaluation-harness restricted to closed multiple-choice benchmarks.
Nsight Systems for benchmark profiling, lm-evaluation-harness for safety rails, and LLM-as-judge for throughput testing.
NeMo Guardrails as the benchmarking harness, with human evaluation used to decontaminate the public benchmark sets.

cards.front: Which metrics evaluate the retrieval stage of a RAG pipeline, separate from generation?
cards.back: Retrieval is scored with ranking metrics: Recall@k (did the relevant document appear in the top k), MRR (mean reciprocal rank of the first relevant hit), NDCG (graded relevance discounted by rank), and Precision@k. These measure whether the right context was fetched, independent of whether the LLM then used it faithfully — so a low end-to-end score can be localized to retrieval versus generation.
cards.quizAnswer: Ranking metrics — Recall@k, MRR, NDCG, Precision@k — measuring whether the right context was retrieved, independent of generation.
distractors: Generation metrics — faithfulness, relevance, and completeness — since retrieval quality is judged by the final answer's grounding.
BLEU and ROUGE-L between the retrieved chunks and the gold answer, measuring how much the context overlaps the response.
Perplexity of the query under the retriever's encoder, since lower query perplexity indicates more relevant documents are fetched.
Ranking metrics like Recall@k and MRR, but only after the LLM has generated, since retrieval cannot be scored on its own.
Latency@k and throughput@k, which measure how quickly the top k documents are returned by the vector database.
Embedding cosine similarity averaged over the corpus, since a higher mean similarity means retrieval is more accurate.

cards.front: What does slice (stratified) analysis add over a single aggregate eval score?
cards.back: It breaks performance down by meaningful subgroups — input length, topic, language, difficulty, or user segment — revealing failures that a single average hides. A model can post a strong overall score while badly underperforming on a critical slice (e.g., long inputs or a specific language). Slicing localizes weaknesses so fixes and regression tests can target the right cases.
cards.quizAnswer: It breaks performance down by subgroup (length, topic, language, difficulty), exposing critical failures a single average hides.
distractors: It averages scores across many random data splits, tightening the confidence interval around the single aggregate metric.
It re-runs the eval at several temperatures and reports the best slice, showing the model's ceiling under ideal decoding.
It splits the eval set into train and test halves, so the aggregate score can be cross-validated for stability.
It breaks performance down by subgroup, but only across model versions, to track how the aggregate score drifts over time.
It samples slices of the production traffic to estimate latency percentiles, complementing the offline accuracy score.
It partitions the model's parameters into slices and evaluates each, localizing which layers cause the most errors.
